chunks.text:
## [§2.2.a] Gym Launch — the $10M ad and the LBO-model problem

Four types of offers that create a money model. Let's dive in. So number one is how we broke the gym industry, and then we'll go into the four-pronged money model, the four components, the four types of offers that exist. All right? So how we broke the gym industry. So I used to get gyms full. So what I just talked about earlier was my model. at Gym Launch. But what created this, and the reason I'm telling you this story is to show you how many different industries this structure of creating a good money model, works in. And so I used to open gyms at full. capacity on day one. And so this, is a video. This was my first... This video, this ad made me 10 million dollars. So it was sick. I' was 26 and it was awesome. Uh, it is how you would imagine it sounded. Um, I honestly did feel like it was something I was doing was illegal. I found out later it's completely legal to advertise and make money. It just sometimes the stars align. And so what I would do is I would go into a neighborhood and, you know, this one I did 191 sign-ups in 19 days at 500 bucks a pop. And this is me holding up all the $500 contracts that I had sold, like in the middle of nowhere. All right? And so this is the old way that gyms worked and the reason I'm, I'm outlining this is 'cause this might be how your business works. And in the gym industry, this is what all the gurus at the time were teaching. It was something that they called the LBO model, which has nothing to do with leveraged buyouts. An LBO model in the gym world was a low barrier offer. All right? So it's like trials, uh, super discount things. But the thing is, is I talked about earlier having a discount thing as the front end, but then they didn't have anything else to sell. So the discount stuff works, but you gotta have the thing to sell to make your money back. But this is how it used to work.

You figure out, okay, the people have come in.
chunks.text:
## [§7.1.b] The 5-step build process (attraction → upsell → downsell → continuity → stack)

So let's go through steps for making your own. So step one is you start with an attraction offer. It al- always starts with getting people to engage, getting leads to engage with you. Step two is you pick an upsell offer. You figure out, okay, the people have come in. They're coming off win-your-money-back. They're coming on, uh, buy X get Y free. They're coming on, uh, decoy offer. Whatever it is, they're coming on the front end. You say, "Great, now I'm either gonna do a menu upsell, I'm gonna do an anchor- anchor upsell, I'm gonna do a rollover upsell." All of those things you can use as your second, second piece. Then you can pick your downsell offer. So what if they say no to any of these other offers? Well, we have our. entire downsell flow, which is we start with payment plans, uh, then we do a free trial, or we do payment plan to feature downsell. Then we go to free trial. You just pick the ones that you know that you can execute on right? now. Step four, then you' pick a continuity offer. And the result is. that you get your cash back in 30 days, more customers, and make more money.

chunks.preview: the most expensive thing first. If the customer balks, you offer a much cheaper and still acceptable alternative: "No worries. You don't care about X, this may be a better fit for you." D, rollover upsells, you credit some or all of a customer's previous purchase towards your next offer. "Since you already spent $500, I'll just credit that towards staying a
chunks.text: the most expensive thing first. If the customer balks, you offer a much cheaper and still acceptable alternative: "No worries. You don't care about X, this may be a better fit for you." D, rollover upsells, you credit some or all of a customer's previous purchase towards your next offer. "Since you already spent $500, I'll just credit that towards staying a full year." Pick your price, which I ended up not including this. I'll probably make a YouTube video about it. Downsell offers are whatever you offer after someone says no. That's my scientific, uh, answer for that. And by turning nos into yeses, you make more money. We covered three of my favorites. So payment plan downsells, you offer the same product at the same price, but they pay some now and the rest over time: "When do you get paid? Let's do half now and half then." Trial with penalty, you let customers try your product or service for free so long as they meet your terms. If they do, then they have a better chance of becoming paying customers. If they don't, they pay. "If you do X, Y, and Z, I'll let you start for free." Feature downsells, you lower prices by changing what the customer gets: "I offer lower quantity, lower quality, or lower priced alternatives, or cut optional components altogether." "If you're okay without a guarantee, I can knock off $400." That's how that works. Then we had continuity offers, which provide ongoing value that customers make ongoing payments for until they cancel. These boost the profit of every customer and give you one last thing to sell. We covered three of my favorites. Continuity bonus offers, so with a continuity bonus, you give the customer an awesome thing if they sign up today. Typically, the bonus itself is more value than the first continuity payment: "If you sign up today, you'll also get XYZ valuable thing." And if you want them to stick, you can continue to give them bonuses to maintain the service. Continuity discount offers we cover is the second type. We covered first month, backend commitments. And you give the customer free time now, an hour later, if they sign up today. And so the key point is that there's four different places that you can spread it out, at the front, at the end, in the middle, in between. That's the point , spread out over the whole thing. But that is fundamentally how continuity offers work to get people to sign up. Then we have the wave fee offers. So first, you ask a customer to pay a startup fee as part of adjoining or month-to-month program.

## [§7.1.c] Implementation rules — perfect one at a time, simple scales, affiliate gap-fill

So here's some notes. One, perfect one of these. at a time. If you try and do all four at once, or six of these things at once, you're- you're gonna break your business. Like, I strongly recommend just start with one. One is enough. There's already enough complexity doing anything new that you haven't done before. I try to include as many important notes as I know that make these things work in these trainings and in the book. There's some things that are in this tr- uh, video training that aren't in the book. There's some things in the book that aren't in the video training. It's the way it is. Uh, so read both if you're really serious about it. But if you have enough from either of them, by all means, please take action. All right, now if you are going to, uh, start one of these things, start it with a lower price. Get people to say yes, and then start raising prices in stages. That's my recommendation. Payment plans, based on how I told you to present the, uh, the discounted rate, uh, basically the pre-payment discount versus the payment plan rate, is a great way to get kind of a look into the future of what prices the market will sustain. I like this little saying, "Simple scales, fancy fails." I wanna have as few of these as humanly possible, because the fewer pieces I have in my business, the fewer things can break. And so I would rather start with the simplest possible. thing that accomplished the objective, which is that I can offset my cost to acquire customers. I can recycle that cash within 30 days, and I keep, keep scaling, basically removing acquisition as the bottleneck in my business. Once I've accomplished that, we've accomplished our objective. We no longer need outside money to scale, which is pretty sweet. So this is a little bit of an advanced tip. Affiliate products can fill money model gaps. So if you're like, "Okay, well, I wanna do a buy X get Y free," then just do a buy X get Y free on someone else's product. So then you. can still sell, your main thing, and then use somebody else's product that, you send traffic to, so you can get cash and you have no operational constraints. Now, you can use that for complementary services rather than something that's gonna take away from your core offer. You can turn attraction offers into continuity offers automatically just with renewal. Buy X get Y free, for example, if I said buy six get six, it becomes a continuity offer if after the end of that first year it rolls into continuity. If I do win your money back and say, "Hey, after this period, you then roll into continuity." If I say decoy offer, I could say, "Hey, you'd pay- you can either do this, uh, you know, this- this simple thing that's not that good, or this amazing thing for more money that has a guarantee." After that period, it rolls into continuity. So you can take any attraction offer that I had and just immediately turn it into continuity by just saying, adding a clause that says it rolls into continuity at. this price. And again, you can mix and match these however you like. So I- I present them in- this order because this is the most logical order that I can think of and as I have used them in my businesses. But this is no- in no way a limitation on your- ability to innovate. And honestly, I want you, I want you to send me all the cool money- models you guys figure out. Like, that's the point. This is me just showing you all the stuff that I know that I could fit into a presentation within a reasonable amount of time. Um, and so again, right, you can use attraction offers and upsell, anything that you can, that you can put your mind to, you can figure out. Okay, so that is how you make the money model. And let's go to 10 years in 10 minutes.

A couple, couple little details for you. Um, talk to your law people about law things. Uh, make sure that they're legal in your area, the how you do it. Eligibility criteria get you more customers to buy the promotional offer. So I ask questions like, to, you know, just to get ammo for a sales conversation. "So why should we pick you? Why this program? Why now? Why does this matter to you? Why is this important? What are your goals?" Right? These are all important questions you can ask in an application that then you can cipher, you know, go through them and say, "Oh, these are really good people." And you can also order the leads and score them based on who you think is more likely to buy. You can also ask for income. You can ask for whatever you want. Like, "How did this not work for you?" And they told me the grand prize was free tickets to their event, which was like $300 for the tickets for the event, and this is a B2B business with a, like, $40,000 thing. And so I was like, "So why do we think that $300 value is in any w-" like, if we're gonna sell $40,000 stuff, we need to have $40,000 giveaways at minimum. And so, like, giveaways are only grand if you have grand prizes, and they have to be super grand because people have to want them, right? Um, here's a little pro tip for you. If a person you refer gets in the top 10, you get to come too. So they won prizes. And so the whole point is if you double your grand prizes, then what you can do is, "Hey, I'm gonna do this grand prize winner," but if you're the person who referred the grand prize winner, then you win a grand prize too. So let's say in that, in that example with the equipment, I would say, "Okay, instead of $50,000 worth of equipment, I'm gonna give two $50,000 packages," or you can give two $25,000 packages, whatever, two big packages, and so that then gets-... every single person who's coming into the contest to say, "Wait, I can get way more chances to win?" And if you can't do it, you can't reach out to those, to 200 leads in, in any more than seven days, then it's like cap it at seven, 207, done. Which leads to the next one, urgency, urgency, urgency, right? So I add urgency in three places for giveaways. Number one is to enter, number two is to claim, number t- three is to use or to, um, to use the, the thing 'cause even once you have, let's say, a scholarship. that you give away or once you give someone a vacation, give them, a time period to use it in, right? And then once you let them know what they won, tell them how long they have to use it. I like hours personally, but I've gone up to five days. In short, just always have deadlines. At the start of the call, let them know that they have two ways to use their partial scholarship, their g- voucher, their gift card, their credit, whatever, all right, and that you'll help them find which way makes the most sense for them, and then offer the discount on the grand prize. thing first. final_answer:
A front-end offer is the first, easy yes you put in front of a prospect. In plain English: it is the small, low-friction thing that gets the right person to raise their hand before you ask them to buy the bigger core offer.

For 1584, the STR Design Diagnostic is the front-end offer: it lets an owner experience your judgment through a listing/photo audit before committing to the full-service design project. The important part is that it should not be a random cheap thing. It should create demand and point naturally to the next sale, because a discounted front end only works when there is something else to sell behind it [cac:12] [offer-types:0]. In the Money Models build order, this is the attraction offer that gets leads engaged before upsells, downsells, continuity, and the full stack [make-your-money-model:1].
